jokes.text: A little more Magnum, PI, a little less Higgins.
jokes.setup: Family pageant with someone commenting on swimsuit choices
jokes.punchline: Comparing looks to the mustached detective vs his British valet
jokes.explanation: Sharp cultural reference that perfectly captures Lucille's cutting commentary style. Well-structured comparison joke with good economy.

jokes.text: Is that Stan Sitwell's eyebrow?
jokes.setup: Someone wearing what appears to be a fake eyebrow
jokes.punchline: Recognition that it belongs to a recurring character known for his hair pieces
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that rewards longtime viewers. The absurdity of recognizing someone's eyebrow is peak Arrested Development.

jokes.text: Get that off your face, you horse's ass.
jokes.setup: Discovering someone is wearing Stan Sitwell's eyebrow
jokes.punchline: Lucille's characteristically harsh insult
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille insult delivery. Not particularly original but perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I mean, I'm just turning 14. And Michael is, too, because they're twins.
jokes.setup: Lindsay claiming it's not a big birthday
jokes.punchline: Revealing she thinks she's turning 14 when she's clearly middle-aged
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of Lindsay's delusion about her age. The twin detail makes it even more absurd and character-specific.

jokes.text: I'm Gob. Uh... Why did you name him that?
jokes.setup: Gob introducing himself to his own grandmother
jokes.punchline: Grandmother's immediate criticism of his name
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing the family's dysfunction. The immediate criticism is both cruel and funny.

jokes.text: Oh, please, you were never little. Your mother was never little.
jokes.setup: Lucille complaining she never got to wear the sash when little
jokes.punchline: Grandmother's cutting response about her never being little
jokes.explanation: Harsh but effective character comedy. Shows how the cruel wit runs in the family.

jokes.text: They didn't have one with a boy doing push-ups.
jokes.setup: Discussion of trophy with a magician on it
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific alternative trophy request
jokes.explanation: Mildly amusing absurdist detail. Not particularly strong but fits the character's odd specificity.

jokes.text: Jack dumped me. He says he wants to start a clean slate on his 80th birthday.
jokes.setup: Explaining why she's not taking a cruise with Uncle Jack
jokes.punchline: Being dumped by an 80-year-old for wanting a 'clean slate'
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about elderly romance. The 'clean slate' detail adds absurdity to the rejection.

jokes.text: My God, when he was three years old, you still had him on a leash! I said, 'She's gonna make a murderer out of that one.'
jokes.setup: Discussing Buster's overprotective upbringing
jokes.punchline: Prophetic prediction about making him a murderer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that works on multiple levels. The foreshadowing is both funny and eerily accurate given the current trial.

jokes.text: Never too early to get Buster used to being in front of a judge.
jokes.setup: Suggesting she can judge the children's pageant
jokes.punchline: Dark joke about preparing Buster for his murder trial
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect timing. The casual way she connects a children's pageant to a murder trial is masterful.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm Chuck's son. No. You're little CJ?
jokes.setup: Recognition of the bailiff as someone from past trials
jokes.punchline: Discovering he's the grown-up son of their former bailiff
jokes.explanation: Nice callback showing the family's extensive history with the legal system. Reinforces their criminal tendencies.

jokes.text: I'm growing dreads, Mother. Dreads right out of my head.
jokes.setup: Lucille noting Buster looks different
jokes.punchline: Buster's explanation that he's growing dreadlocks 'out of his head'
jokes.explanation: Classic Buster line with his characteristic literal and odd phrasing. The redundancy of 'out of my head' is perfectly Buster.

jokes.text: Just because you have an African-American left hand, all of a sudden you're Ray Charles?
jokes.setup: Buster claiming to grow dreads
jokes.punchline: Lucille's typically inappropriate racial observation about his transplanted hand
jokes.explanation: Peak Lucille inappropriate commentary. References the transplant storyline while making an absurd cultural assumption.

jokes.text: And I thought you Alibabaed one that was less... threatening.
jokes.setup: Continuing discussion about Buster's new hand
jokes.punchline: Using 'Alibaba' as a verb for online shopping
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing Lucille's confusion about modern technology. 'Alibabaed' as a verb is inventive and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: The so-called Guilty Guys were a law firm whose record was so unblemished... they'd even been the subject of a reality show.
jokes.setup: Introducing the Guilty Guys law firm
jokes.punchline: Their 'unblemished' record leading to a reality show (when they only defend guilty clients)
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup for the lawyers' gimmick. The meta-reference to reality shows adds layer.

jokes.text: The project, which was to show a single case over the span of a season... was abandoned early on by Netflix as not being binge-worthy.
jokes.setup: Describing the reality show about the lawyers
jokes.punchline: Netflix canceling it for not being binge-worthy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about Netflix's own viewing patterns, particularly relevant since AD itself is on Netflix.

jokes.text: I'm gonna try and get a selfie with the G-Man.
jokes.setup: Barry explaining why he's at the trial
jokes.punchline: Treating a serious legal proceeding like celebrity spotting
jokes.explanation: Classic Barry inappropriate behavior, treating serious situation casually

jokes.text: They're going to be mildly displeased.
jokes.setup: Warning about Chinese investors' reaction to Michael spending their money
jokes.punchline: Understating potential violent retaliation as 'mildly displeased'
jokes.explanation: Perfect understatement given the context of receiving severed body parts. Classic AD deadpan delivery.

jokes.text: George Michael felt bulldozed by the announcement.
jokes.setup: George Michael's reaction to Michael's wall announcement
jokes.punchline: Using 'bulldozed' literally (wall construction) and figuratively (feeling overwhelmed)
jokes.explanation: Clever narrator pun that works on multiple levels. Typical AD wordplay sophistication.

jokes.text: Mum's the word for 'severed human foot' in Chinese.
jokes.setup: Gob explaining that Michael was told to keep the foot secret
jokes.punchline: Claiming 'mum's the word' literally translates to 'severed human foot'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that transforms an idiom into a literal translation. Peak AD linguistic comedy with perfect Gob delivery.

jokes.text: Mine's chained to a bench. So, in that sense, he's an overachiever.
jokes.setup: Discussion about children trying to embarrass their parents
jokes.punchline: Calling Buster an overachiever for being chained up for murder
jokes.explanation: Masterful dark comedy with perfect Lucille delivery. The 'overachiever' characterization of murder charges is brilliantly twisted.

jokes.text: It was hard for anyone not to get caught up in the pageantry.
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys making a dramatic entrance
jokes.punchline: Using 'pageantry' to describe legal proceedings
jokes.explanation: Subtle wordplay connecting the family pageant to the courtroom drama

jokes.text: Your Honor, in the case of The State of California v. Buster Bluth, we'd like to enter a plea of... guilty.
jokes.setup: Formal legal proceeding with expectation of not guilty plea
jokes.punchline: The defense immediately pleading guilty
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of legal drama expectations. The pause before 'guilty' builds perfect tension.

jokes.text: Look at that. There is always a guy who wants to know what's going on. It's why I could never go to the movies with my father.
jokes.setup: Michael's outburst about the guilty plea
jokes.punchline: Judge's personal tangent about movie-going with his father
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor that breaks courtroom tension with relatable personal anecdote.

jokes.text: They've never technically won a case... but they've never lost a fee.
jokes.setup: Discussion of the Guilty Guys' track record
jokes.punchline: Distinguishing between legal success and financial success
jokes.explanation: Sharp parallel structure joke about lawyer priorities. The wordplay between 'case' and 'fee' is elegant.

jokes.text: she called for a mistrial
jokes.setup: DA being flustered by ex-husband's argument
jokes.punchline: Professional prosecutor so emotionally compromised she calls mistrial
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the legal strategy. Shows professional competence being undermined by personal issues.

jokes.text: I'm out of context again, aren't I?
jokes.setup: George Sr. giving advice about checking into hotels with a male companion
jokes.punchline: Realizing his advice doesn't apply to the current situation
jokes.explanation: Good meta-moment where character acknowledges narrative confusion. Classic AD self-awareness.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. Yeah, we had to do some crazy vote-out thing where he... he forgot how voting worked. He voted for himself.
jokes.setup: Discussing getting rid of unwanted roommates
jokes.punchline: Michael's confusion about voting leading to self-elimination
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Michael's incompetence at basic democratic processes.

jokes.text: You sound just like a slightly self-aware Aunt Lindsay.
jokes.setup: George Michael expressing concern for immigrant families
jokes.punchline: Comparing him to Lindsay but with minimal self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Sharp character observation. The 'slightly self-aware' qualifier is a perfect insult for both Lindsay and George Michael.

jokes.text: Well, you sound just like a minimally-woke Uncle Michael.
jokes.setup: Maeby's comparison to Lindsay
jokes.punchline: George Michael's parallel insult using 'minimally-woke'
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that mirrors Maeby's structure while capturing Michael's political personality.

jokes.text: But, hey, maybe you can get those dumb attorneys from that short Netflix show that's not on the air anymore. They canceled that?
jokes.setup: Maeby sarcastically suggesting the Guilty Guys
jokes.punchline: George Michael learning the show was canceled
jokes.explanation: Continues the meta commentary on Netflix while advancing the plot

jokes.text: They canceled that?
jokes.setup: Maeby mentioning the Netflix show about the Guilty Guys
jokes.punchline: George Michael's surprise at the cancellation
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that acknowledges the meta-joke from earlier. Brief but effective.

jokes.text: those dumb attorneys from that short Netflix show that's not on the air anymore
jokes.setup: Suggesting legal representation
jokes.punchline: Dismissive description of the Guilty Guys show that was just canceled
jokes.explanation: Solid callback but feels somewhat forced. The meta humor is expected at this point.

jokes.text: Well, maybe, like, babies, but that's weird, because who likes babies?
jokes.setup: Discussing Lottie's lack of interest in children
jokes.punchline: Dusty's casual dismissal of babies as inherently unlikable
jokes.explanation: Mildly subversive take on universal baby appreciation. Shows Dusty's contrarian nature.

jokes.text: Right. That's why I chose the word 'weird.'
jokes.setup: Lucille noting she's old enough to be his mother after he calls their potential relationship weird
jokes.punchline: Dusty's matter-of-fact acknowledgment of the age-inappropriate nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that acknowledges the uncomfortable age gap. Economy of language makes it funnier.

jokes.text: It's Lucille Austero. I've been looking for you. I know you haven't forgotten...
jokes.setup: Michael claiming Lucille 2 calls him all the time
jokes.punchline: Playing same recorded message, implying she's actually dead
jokes.explanation: Dark revelation about Michael's cover-up, delivered through repetitive messages

jokes.text: I just wasted an hour and a half working on an opening joke, till I realized that it's from Fast and Furious. It doesn't even work unless you're in a Camaro.
jokes.setup: Dusty stressed about his opening statement
jokes.punchline: Accidentally plagiarizing an action movie for court
jokes.explanation: Good absurd detail about inappropriate source material for legal proceedings

jokes.text: I... I'm talking about Buster.
jokes.setup: Dusty's rant about Lucille always talking about Buster
jokes.punchline: Lucille clarifying she meant her actual son, not calling Dusty her son
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that clears up the confusion while maintaining the awkwardness of their relationship.

jokes.text: I hate him! I hope he loses! I'm going to my fort!
jokes.setup: Dusty's complete breakdown during argument
jokes.punchline: Childish tantrum ending with running to his 'fort'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character breakdown that shows Dusty's immaturity. The 'fort' detail adds to the childishness.

jokes.text: Hey! Locals only, brosef. Turista non grata, dude.
jokes.setup: Dusty being rejected from the beach fort
jokes.punchline: Surfer's mix of 'persona non grata' with tourist terminology
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay combining legal Latin with surfer slang. Shows the surfers' territorial attitude.

jokes.text: Huh. That doesn't necessarily mean yours has to take the night off.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Dusty keeping his head in his work
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s sexual suggestion about Lucille
jokes.explanation: Crude but character-appropriate innuendo. Shows George Sr.'s approach to problem-solving.

jokes.text: Three guesses what she did to get on two.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Mrs. Salazar moving from pantry captain to second floor
jokes.punchline: Implications about how she earned her promotion
jokes.explanation: Standard innuendo joke without much originality. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: How rich is this sand hobo?
jokes.setup: Discovering Dusty has an extensive household staff
jokes.punchline: Lucille's characteristically cutting description of him as a 'sand hobo'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille insult with brilliant economy. 'Sand hobo' captures both his beach lifestyle and her class snobbery.

jokes.text: You've taken all the fun out of the worst part of life.
jokes.setup: Tobias voting himself out of the house
jokes.punchline: Describing living with family as 'the worst part of life' but complaining it's no longer fun
jokes.explanation: Excellent paradoxical observation about family dysfunction. Perfectly captures Tobias's dramatic but logical perspective.
